# Coming-of-Age Films — Theme Guide

## What Coming-of-Age Means in Cinema
Coming-of-age films — the Bildungsroman in cinematic form — track the moment when a young person's understanding of the world is permanently and irreversibly changed. The best of them are not nostalgic: they show growing up as a process of loss, disillusionment, and hard-won clarity. They're among the most emotionally durable films in cinema because everyone has been young, and the experience of recognising one's own formation never ages.

## The Classics

**The 400 Blows (1959)** — Rating: 8.2. François Truffaut. Semi-autobiographical. A Parisian boy trapped between a neglectful home and an uncomprehending school system. The final freeze frame — the boy's face at the sea's edge — is one of cinema's most famous shots.

**To Kill a Mockingbird (1962)** — Rating: 8.3. Gregory Peck as Atticus Finch. Scout and Jem's moral education in Depression-era Alabama. Boo Radley. The trial. The film that defined what decency looked like in America.

**Stand by Me (1986)** — Rating: 8.1. Rob Reiner. Stephen King adaptation. Four boys walk to see a dead body. A film about male friendship and the moment you realise childhood is ending. "I never had any friends later on like the ones I had when I was twelve."

**Dead Poets Society (1989)** — Rating: 8.1. Robin Williams as a charismatic English teacher at a repressive boarding school. "O Captain, My Captain." Carpe diem. The standing-on-desks ending.

**My Neighbor Totoro (1988)** — Rating: 8.1. Hayao Miyazaki. Two girls move to the countryside while their mother is ill. Totoro. The Catbus. One of the purest depictions of childhood wonder in cinema.

## The 1990s–2000s

**Boyz n the Hood (1991)** — Rating: 7.8. John Singleton. Three friends growing up in South Central Los Angeles. A coming-of-age film about the structures (poverty, gang violence, absent fathers) that make youth impossible.

**Dazed and Confused (1993)** — Rating: 7.6. Richard Linklater. The last day of school in Austin, Texas, 1976. About the rituals of adolescence — the hazing, the parties, the sense that this moment is everything and nothing.

**The Virgin Suicides (1999)** — Rating: 7.2. Sofia Coppola. Five sisters in suburban Michigan, observed by the neighbourhood boys who love them. About female subjectivity as something permanently mysterious to men.

**Amélie (2001)** — Rating: 8.3. A coming-of-age film about a shy adult woman in Paris who has kept the world at arm's length since childhood.

**Whale Rider (2002)** — Rating: 7.4. A Māori girl in New Zealand who believes she is destined to lead her people. A study in tradition, gender, and the cost of belonging.

**Thirteen (2003)** — Rating: 7.4. Catherine Hardwicke. A good student falls in with the popular crowd. A film about how quickly early adolescence can go wrong, and how much the adults around children miss.

**Kings of Summer (2013)** — Rating: 7.2. Three boys build a house in the woods and declare their independence. Bittersweet and funny.

**The Spectacular Now (2013)** — Rating: 7.1. Two teenagers fall in love. One is an alcoholic. About the romance of self-destruction and the first person who sees through it.

## Modern Masterworks

**Boyhood (2014)** — Rating: 7.9. Richard Linklater. Shot over twelve years with the same actors as a boy grows from six to eighteen. The passage of time is not simulated: you watch it happen. Nothing quite like it exists.

**The Perks of Being a Wallflower (2012)** — Rating: 7.9. A new high school student navigates his first year. Logan Lerman, Emma Watson, Ezra Miller. About how the damaged recognize each other.

**Moonlight (2016)** — Rating: 7.4. Barry Jenkins. Three chapters from the life of a Black gay man in Miami. About the formation of identity under conditions of violence and poverty. Won Best Picture.

**Lady Bird (2017)** — Rating: 7.4. Greta Gerwig's semi-autobiographical debut. A Sacramento teenager desperate to escape her hometown and her mother. Saoirse Ronan. Laurie Metcalf. One of the most emotionally precise films about the mother-daughter relationship.

**Call Me By Your Name (2017)** — Rating: 7.8. Luca Guadagnino. A summer in Italy. A seventeen-year-old and his father's research assistant fall in love. About how the best summers contain the seeds of their own ending. Armie Hammer and Timothée Chalamet. The peach scene.

**Eighth Grade (2018)** — Rating: 7.4. Bo Burnham. A thirteen-year-old girl's last week of middle school. The most accurate depiction of contemporary adolescence — the anxiety, the phone, the gap between self-presentation and interior life — ever made.

**The Florida Project (2017)** — Rating: 7.7. Sean Baker. A six-year-old living in a budget motel outside Disney World. The end of childhood arrives whether or not you're ready.

**Mid90s (2018)** — Rating: 7.3. Jonah Hill's directorial debut. A thirteen-year-old finds his tribe in a Los Angeles skate crew. Shot on 16mm. Deeply felt.

## International Coming-of-Age

**Y Tu Mamá También (2001)** — Rating: 7.6. Alfonso Cuarón. Two teenage boys and an older woman drive across Mexico. About class, sexuality, and the obliviousness of privilege.

**Spirited Away (2001)** — Rating: 8.6. Hayao Miyazaki. A ten-year-old girl enters the spirit world to rescue her parents. The greatest coming-of-age fantasy ever made.

**Son of Saul (2015)** — Rating: 7.5. A Sonderkommando in Auschwitz who believes he has found his son's body. Coming-of-age as moral reckoning.

**Shoplifters (2018)** — Rating: 7.9. Hirokazu Kore-eda. A found family of outcasts in Tokyo. A film about the families we choose and the ones we're born into.

## For a Coming-of-Age Marathon
**Foundation:** Stand by Me, Dead Poets Society, The 400 Blows
**Modern Classics:** Boyhood, Moonlight, Lady Bird, Call Me By Your Name
**Under-seen:** Eighth Grade, The Florida Project, Dazed and Confused
